observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the General
Services Officer to finish the conduct of physical count of PPE and prepare Property
Cards for each category of PPE, in compliance with Sections 124 and 119 of the
MNGAS, Volume I, to ascertain existence and valuation of the property and establish
accountability thereon. Also, coordinate with the Municipal Accountant for the
reconciliation of records of the PPE and submit the RPCPPE to the Auditor for
verification.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to maintain subsidiary ledger/details of the account, after which
depreciate the same in compliance with existing regulations to facilitate the validation
of account balances.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the:
 Accountable Officers (AO) and employees to liquidate their cash advances,
otherwise deduct the corresponding amounts from the money due them; and
 Municipal Accountant not to grant additional cash advance to those with
outstanding balance and see to it that advances are liquidated at the end of the
year for fair presentation of financial statements.

Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor secure authority from the
Sangguniang Bayan (SB) to request from the COA the write-off of the unliquidated
cash advances of those AO whose whereabouts can no longer be traced in
accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor make representation with
the SB to pass a Resolution authorizing him to file a request from COA for the write-
off of the accounts. Upon approval of the Resolution, file the request for the write-off
of the accounts to the Commission on Audit in pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-
005.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct
the Municipal Accountant to maintain complete sets of subsidiary ledgers for all
public utilities and economic enterprises operated by the Municipality including the
loans and 20 percent DF and to prepare the required FS in compliance with Section
313 of RA No. 7160 and Sections 105 and 110 of the MNGAS.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer to:
 submit to the SA and ATL at the beginning of the year, the list of all ongoing
government projects/programs/activities and those that are to be implemented
during the year;
 inform the Auditors within 10 days after the award of the infrastructure project or
before the start of the program/activity that the appropriate project signboards
and/or public notices were already posted for validation; and
 submit the monthly monitoring report in compliance with the above cited
regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Bids and Awards Committee
(BAC) Secretariat to submit to the Audit Team the copies of the contracts together
with the supporting documents within five working days from approval thereof. Also,
instruct the BAC to strictly adhere and comply with the pertinent provisions of COA
Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 on the prompt submission of perfected
contracts and its supporting documents, to ensure timely review, evaluation and
verification thereof.

We recommended that the ICO-Municipal Accountant and the ICO-
Municipal Treasurer give priority to the timely submission of the accounts
and year-end financial reports within the prescribed period required under
COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009, COA Circular No.
95-006 dated May 18, 1995 and Circular No. 2010-001 and Paragraph 2 of
Section 41 of PD 1445 to enable the Audit Team to conduct timely audit
thereof.
We also recommend that the Municipal Mayor look into the possible
cause of the delayed preparation and submission of the said reports and take
immediate action to solve this problem.

We recommended that the ICO-Municipal Accountant prepare and
submit to the Audit Team the Subsidiary Schedules of GL accounts to
substantiate the validity and correctness of the accounts in the financial
statements, pursuant to Sections 111.1 and 114.2 of PD 1445 and Sections 2
and 4.1 of COA Circular No. 2010-001 dated March 2, 2010.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through its
Inventory Committee, conduct complete physical count of the
properties of the Municipality, reconcile the results of the count
with the accounting records and submit the complete and accurate
RPCPPE to the Audit Team, pursuant to Section 124 of the
Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant observe
the proper accounting for transfer of PPE accounts. Prepare the
necessary adjustment to correct the balances of the affected
accounts in the Statement of Financial Position of General Fund.
We recommended that the ICO-Municipal Accountant
effect the prompt transfer of the cost of completed project from
the Trust Fund to the General Fund books.
We recommended that the ICO- Municipal Accountant
effect the necessary adjusting entry for the transfer of the costs of
the completed projects from CIP accounts to Infrastructure Assets
and Buildings and Other Structures accounts.
We recommended that the ICO-Municipal Accountant
provide depreciation for all depreciable Property, Plant and
Equipment (PPE) as provided under Section 4(o) of the NGAS
Manuel for LGU, Volume 1 to establish proper valuation of PPE
accounts.

We recommended that the ICO-Municipal Accountant:
a. strictly observe the monthly preparation of Bank Reconciliation
Statements to validate the balance of the Cash in Bank account in
39
the financial statements, in compliance with Section 3 of COA
Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996;
b. exert diligent effort in reconciling the book and bank balances and
submit the BRS and other relevant documents showing that the
identified reconciling items were properly recorded and adjusted
in the books of accounts;
c. reconcile accounting records with the cash book of the ICO-
Municipal Treasurer. Henceforth, both offices should reconcile
their records regularly so that errors or discrepancies that may be
noted could be readily adjusted; and
d. maintain separate subsidiary ledgers for each depository account
to substantiate the validity and correctness of the balances of each
account and to easily monitor the transactions affecting those
accounts.

In view of the foregoing audit observations, we recommended that:
a) the ICO-Municipal Treasurer maintain cashbook by fund and
regularly update and reconcile records with the cash on hand daily or
as necessary. Also, reconcile the cashbooks with the accounting
records at least monthly to ensure that the recorded transactions are
complete and accurate.
b) the collecting officers observe daily remittance of collections and
preparation and submission of corresponding RCDs to the ICO-
Municipal Treasurer to avoid delays in the reporting of cash
collections and remittances.
c) the ICO-Municipal Accountant ensure the completeness and accuracy
of the recorded transactions. Adjust the balances of the accounts
affected by the miscalculated collections and deposits.
We recommended that the concerned collecting officer
submit written explanation relative to the above deficiencies noted.
We also recommended that the ICO-Municipal Treasurer
verify the remittances of collections of the accountable officers
before acknowledging receipt of the submitted RCD to ensure that
all collections are remitted properly and intact.
Additionally, we recommended that the ICO-Municipal
Accountant, being the internal auditor of the Municipality review
the official receipts and deposit slips supporting the Report of
Collections and Deposits submitted by the Office of the Municipal
Treasurer prior to recording the same in the books to warrant
that the report and its supporting documents are complete and
valid.
We recommended that the collecting officers and the ICO-
Municipal Treasurer comply strictly with the provisions of
Sections 50 and 51 of the Manual on NGAS, Volume II. Prepare
and the submit the Consolidated Report of Accountability for
Accountable Forms together with the Report of Accountability for
Accountable Forms monthly in compliance with the above-cited
provision.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor designate
collecting officer who shall exclusively perform the collecting
function pursuant to Section 50 of GAAM Volume III. The
disbursing officer should not be allowed to perform collecting
functions to avoid mishandling of government funds.
We recommended that the ICO-Municipal Treasurer
ensure adequate bonding of all accountable officers to conform
with the requirements set forth under Section 305(f) of the Local
Government Code of 1991, Section 101(2) of PD 1445 and Section
5.1 of National Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 dated
August 6, 2009.

Based on the foregoing audit observations, we recommended that the
Municipal Mayor:
a. Require all accountable officers and employees to immediately
liquidate their outstanding cash advances;
b. Exert extra efforts to recover outstanding cash advances from
Accountable Officers who are no longer in the service nor
connected with the municipality;
c. Direct the Municipal Accountant to refrain from granting
additional cash advances to any official or employee unless
previous cash advance given to him is first settled or a proper
accounting thereof is made, pursuant to Section 4.1.2 of COA
Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997;
d. Require the officials and employees to liquidate their cash
advances within the prescribed period provided in Section 5 of
COA Circular 97-002.
e. Instruct the ICO Municipal Accountant to Reclassify “Due from
Officers and Employees” account to “Advances to Officers and
Employees” in compliance with the Revised Chart of Account to
present the correct account title in the Financial Statements (FS)
and to trace the discrepancy noted in the balance of cash advances
per book and per audit. Thereafter, prepare the necessary
adjustment to correct the balance presented in the FS.
f. Require strictly an Office Clearance from Money and Property
accountabilities, pursuant to Sections 200 and 256, Vol. I of the
Government Accounting and Auditing Manual from all personnel
who seek retirement, resignation, transfer or claim for death
benefits in order that all accountabilities could be appropriately
settled;

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
a. the ICO-Municipal Accountant to set up RPT/SET
Receivables in accordance with Section 20 of NGAS Manual,
Volume I;
b. the ICO-Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal
Accountant at the beginning of the year with a duly certified
list of Real Property Taxpayers showing the names of
taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year;
and
c. the Municipal Assessor to furnish the Treasurer with the
latest valuations of property of each taxpayer;
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the
Municipal Assessor create strategies to intensify collection of Real
Property Taxes and enforce collection of delinquent RPTs.

We recommended that the ICO Municipal Accountant and the
Municipal Treasurer release to the Barangay Treasurers the barangay
shares from RPT collections in order for the barangay to utilize the fund for
its purpose. Likewise, observe the timely release of RPT shares to barangay
in the succeeding years.

We recommended that the ICO- Municipal Accountant:
a. adhere faithfully to the provisions of Republic Act (R.A.) No. 8291,
Republic Act (R.A.) No. 9679 and Republic Act (R.A.) No. 7875 on
the prompt and full remittance of the amount due to the respective
agencies to avoid incurrence of penalty and interest; and
b. maintain individual subsidiary ledgers for Due to GSIS, Due to
Pag-IBIG and Due to PhilHealth accounts to facilitate ease of
monitoring and reconciliation of balances.
We also recommended that the ICO-Municipal Treasurer reconcile
the remittance advice/list against the payroll to avoid the non-remittance of
the amount withheld from employees

We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) facilitate
establishment and maintenance of GAD database to have an effective basis
for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation and to
undertake thorough gender analysis based on relevant data for proper
identification of existing gender issues to ensure that the approved PPAs to
be incorporated in the GPB are responsive to address the same.

We reiterated our recommendation that the MDRRMO facilitate
proper planning and monitoring of DRRM programs/projects/activities
and see to it that the DRRM fund is optimally utilized for its intended
purpose.
We recommended and the ICO-Municipal Accountant agreed to
ensure that subsequent disbursements are supported with complete
documentation pursuant to Section 4(6) of PD 1445.
We recommended that the ICO-Municipal Accountant:
a. require the school heads for the immediate submission of
utilization report on the donated amount supported by
relevant documents such as official receipts for the purchases
of materials and progress pictures of the proposed projects to
prove that the amount granted were properly utilized for the
purpose.
b. ensure that subsequent donations granted to other
government agencies are properly substantiated with official
receipts to guarantee proper accounting and utilization of
fund.
We recommended that the management submit written
explanation relative to the deficiency noted. See to it that all subsequent
disbursements are supported with appropriations.
We reiterated our prior years’ audit recommendation that the
Municipal Accountant adhere strictly to the accounting and reporting
guidelines for LDRRM Fund set forth under COA Circular No. 2012-
002 dated September 12, 2012 so as to present reliable data in the
LGU’s financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development
Office together with the Local Development Council judiciously and
meticulously plan programs/projects/activities included in the Annual
Investment Plan and promptly implement those priority projects in
order to deliver satisfactory benefits to the constituents.
We reiterated our prior years’ audit recommendation that the
Municipal Mayor and other concerned municipal officials observe
strictly Items 3.0 and 4.0 of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 of
the DILG and DBM. Avoid charging expenses which do not constitute
capital expenditures under the 20% Development Fund.

We recommended that management discontinue the practice of
procuring goods or services on reimbursement basis, except for petty
expenses. Strictly observe the provision of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184
in all government procurement.

We recommend that the Management adhere strictly to the provisions
of RA 9003 to ensure total protection for the constituents as well as the
environment.
Enforce the no segregation/no collection policy so as to compel the
constituents to observe proper segregation of waste;
Provide the garbage collectors with the necessary training relative to
the proper handling of solid wastes; and
Take an initial step to convert the controlled dump site to sanitary
land fill by allocating budget for development projects which may be sourced
from the 20% Development Fund.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive requires:

        1. The Accountant and General  Services  Officer  to  complete  the
           reconciliation process to eliminate  the  discrepancies  in  the
           inventory report and accounting records;


        2. The Accountant to prepare adjusting  and  reclassifying  entries
           on the following:


             a. To recognize in the books items 4 and 5;


             b. To derecognize from the books items 6, 7  and 11; and


             c. To reclassify to appropriate accounts item 8 above.


        3.  The  General  Services  Officer  to   correct   the   erroneous
           classification of PPEs presented in Annex  A  in  his  inventory
           report and transfer recording of all unserviceable items  (Annex
           B) in IIRUP.

     We also recommended the immediate and proper disposal of unserviceable
     properties pursuant to Section 79 of P.D. 1445 and par. V  of  COA  Circular
No. 89-296.

We recommended to the Local Chief Executive to require:


   1. The General Services Officer to prepare and  submit  SSMI  together
      with the original copy of RIS; and


   2. The Municipal Accountant to prepare JEV upon receipt of SSMI &  RIS
      to record adjustments to come up with the correct balances  of  the
      affected accounts.

We recommended that Management  require  the  HRO  together  with  the
concerned DHs of all Offices  or  create  a  committee  that  would  enhance
guidelines on hiring/rehiring of JO Personnel with the end in view of:


    a.1   limiting the number of hired/rehired personnel based  on  actual
        manpower need by each office;


    a.2   assuring that JO Personnel do not perform functions of  existing
        regular employees; and


    a.3   declaring that there will be no duplication of works  among  JOs
        assigned in one Office.

    We recommended further that  such  enhanced  hiring  guidelines  shall
cover  all  LGU  prospective  JO  applicants,  both   from   executive   and
legislative departments, including those to be provided to other Offices.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive  require  the  Municipal
Agriculture Officer to:

   1. adhere strictly on the timelines set in the  MOA.   We  recommended
      that project should  be  properly  planned  to  facilitate  project
      execution and reporting;
   2. closely monitor the implementation of the project relative  to  the
      fund releases by prioritizing and  organizing  project  activities.
      We suggested that the MAO practice time management; and


   3. submit immediately complete accounting of distribution  of  fishing
      gear  paraphernalia  together  with  duly  signed   acknowledgement
      receipts by the beneficiaries.

We recommended to the Local Chief Executive to require  the  Municipal
Treasurer:

   1.  submit all missing disbursement vouchers to the accounting  office
      for proper recording in the books of the LGU;


   2. prepare the RCI containing all checks issued during the period  and
      submit this to the Accounting Office  together  with  corresponding
      DVs;


   3. ensure that complete supporting documents are attached to  the  DVs
      before the paid transactions  are  transmitted  to  the  accounting
      office; and


   4. observe the ninety (90) day  period  within  which  to  submit  the
      missing DVs before suspensions mature into disallowance.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare  and  record
JEV upon receipt of DVs and  supporting  documents  to  adjust  and  correct
balances of the affected accounts.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive  require  the  Municipal
Accountant to secure the bank account numbers of the Source  Agencies  where
the unutilized balances shall be deposited.

We recommended that the Municipality cause the  immediate  closure  of
the LGU’s existing CDF  and  facilitate  the  immediate  construction  of  a
proper sanitary landfill.  Such immediate action is necessary to address  if
not prevent, detrimental  ecological  issues  and  their  effects  on  human
health and to the environment.

     We also recommended that Management furnish  immediately  the  Auditor
with the approved closure plan and ATC pertaining to  the  Palawig  CDF  for
audit purposes.

We recommended that Management:


   1. Adopt the guidelines promulgated by DSWD in the selection  of  AICS
      beneficiaries and in setting up of the maximum  amount  or  ceiling
      for each type or kind of financial assistance;


   2. Ensure that only the DSWD personnel assess and evaluate  applicants
      requesting financial assistance out of AICS program  including  the
      identification of nearest kin who serve as the claimant;


   3. Provide specific budget in the Office of  the  MSWD  for  the  AICS
      program; and


   4.  Require  the  Municipal  Accountant  to  be  more  circumspect  in
      reviewing completeness of supporting documents paying attention  on
      erasures and signatures.

We recommended that the Local Chief  Executive  direct  the  Municipal
Engineer to conduct regular inspection at  barangays  to  identify  building
owners undertaking  construction  of  building  without  necessary  building
permit. Henceforth, require the Building owners to secure  building  permits
in compliance with the  Building  Code  and  have  them  pay  the  necessary
building permit fees as source of additional revenue.

We recommended that the LGU continuously comply with the provision  of
Section  138(c)  of  GAAM,  Volume  I  that  funds  received  from  National
Government Agencies and other sources  shall  be  spent  only  for  specific
purposes it were granted.

We recommended that Management continue  with  the  identification  of
specific developmental projects to be charged against  the  20%  Development
Fund, as envisioned in DILG-DBM Joint  Memorandum  Circular  No.  2017-1  in
order to attain 100 per cent project accomplishment.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Property and
Supply/General Services Treasurer to coordinate with the OIC - Municipal
Accountant for the reconciliation of records of the PPE and submit the
RPCPPE to the Auditor for verification.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Mayor appointed the new Municipal Accountant and
Municipal Accountant started the reconciliation of records of the PPE and
submit the RPCPPE to the Auditor for verification.
observations.reason:
The new GSO and the Municipal Accountant started the physical count of PPE of the
Municipality to determine the number of assets of the Municipality and for other purposes.
They assured compliance of the audit recommendation and will conduct the reconciliation of
accounts with the Accounting Office base on the physical count of PPE the soonest possible
time.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
 Accountable Officers (AO) and employees to liquidate their cash
advances, otherwise deduct the corresponding amounts from the money
due them; and
 Municipal Accountant not to grant additional cash advance to those with
outstanding balance and see to it that advances are liquidated at the end
of the year for fair presentation of financial statements.

Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor secure authority from the

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

direct the submission of MSWMP to the Provincial Solid Waste Management Board for review and for approval by the National Solid Waste Management Commission;

evaluate the current composition of the MSWMB and look for possible members that could qualify to complete the line up; and

stop the operation on open dump site for its garbage disposal, instead, develop a sanitary landfill as final garbage disposal site of the LGU.
observations.reason: For CY 2017, the municipality was not able to comply all the requirements to secure approval of NSWC for its Solid Waste Management Plan. According to the Solid Waste Focal Person, lack of manpower that will  prepare the waste characterization (WACS) of the municipality  is one of the reason why they cannot comply/complete the requirements needed. As to the operation of open dump site, the Municipal Mayor told the Audit Team during the exit conference on March 15, 2018, that the municipality will close the existing dump site this year. Since they have no budget for sanitary landfill they will use  controlled dumpsite wherein the garbage will be covered by soil, until such time that the municipality has already constructed its own MRF with complete machineries/equipment for their solid waste management. According to them, they will ask financial assistance from National Government for the realization of this project.

observations.reason: Same observation was noted by the Audit Team. No comment was given by management.

require the strict and uniform implementation of the provisions of Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 97-002;

require all accountable officers and employees to settle/liquidate all their outstanding cash advances by sending confirmation, notification or demand letters. 

in case of failure to settle/liquidate their cash advances, cause or order the withholding of the payment of their salaries as provided for under Section 9.3.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and impose the sanctions provided under the penal provision of Section 128 of PD No. 1445 and/or study the possibility of filing the appropriate charges to enforce settlement thereof in accordance with Section 9.3.3 of the same Circular and Article 217 of the Revised Penal Code; and 

require an Office Clearance from Money and Property accountabilities, pursuant to Sections 200 and 256, Vol. I of the Government Accounting and Auditing Manual from all personnel who seek retirement, resignation, transfer or claim for death benefits in order that all accountabilities could be appropriately settled.
observations.reason: Same observation was noted in CY 2017. The new OIC-Municipal Accountant stated that Management required/reminded all accountable officers and employees to settle their obligations.

prepare JEVs to adjust/record issuances on the affected accounts and coordinate closely with the Municipal Treasurer to ensure reconciliation of their inventory records, also in respect of physical inventory taking.

regularly prepare and submit accurate and complete Summary of Supplies and Materials Issue (SSMI) with supporting Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) pursuant to Section 121 of the NGAS Manual, Volume I, to the Accounting Unit for proper recording/posting of issuance in the books of accounts and Supplies Ledger Cards (SLCs).

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the ICO – Municipal Accountant strictly
observe the timely submission of the Financial Statements and its supporting
schedules and accounts (DVs/Payrolls/Official Receipts) to the Audit Team.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor look into the
possible cause of the delayed preparation and submission of the said reports
and take immediate action to solve this problem.
The management committed to comply with the above audit
recommendations.

We recommended that the ICO-Municipal Accountant see to it that
claims for travel expenses of the municipal officials and employees are
reasonable and/or within the allowed limit prescribed in Executive Order No.
248 as amended by Executive Order 298.
No comment was received from the newly designated ICO-Municipal
Accountant as the above reimbursements were paid prior to his assumption,
however, he signified compliance with the above audit recommendations for the
ensuing years.

We recommended that the Municipality observe the limit of
granting cash assistance directly to the recipients as required in the
afore-cited memorandum circular.
We also recommended that the Municipality stop the grant of
livelihood assistance to private individuals, instead, properly plan the
implementation of the livelihood programs within the Municipality and
see to it that the entire public enjoys equal benefits therefrom.
The management commented that the Sangguniang Bayan has
already approved a SB resolution which requires SB approval for the grant
of financial assistance above 5,000 up to 19,000.00.
The municipal officials present during the exit conference on
March 15, 2018 could not give further comment relative to the above
deficiencies as the foregoing subject transactions materialized during the
previous administration. However, the incumbent Municipal Mayor
signified compliance with the above audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the
submission of the LDRRMFIP to the Audit Team in compliance with
Item 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the
above mentioned lacking documents to prove the validity, regularity
and propriety of the disbursements and see to it that all disbursements
are supported with complete documents prior to processing for
payment.
We recommended that the MDRRMO facilitate proper planning
and monitoring of DRRM activities and see to it that the DRRM fund is
optimally utilized for its intended purpose.

We recommended that the Local School Board strictly comply with
the provisions of Section 272 of RA 7160 and DECS-DBM-DILG Joint
Circular No. 1 dated January 19, 2017 in the prioritization of programs,
activities and projects that should be included in the LSB Budget and ensure
utilization of the SEF for the purpose prescribed by law.
No comment was received from the Local School Board, however, the
management noted the audit recommendation.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Person observe timely
submission of GAD Plan and Budget to DILG Regional Office for
review and approval in compliance with PCW-DILG-DNM-NEDA
JMC No. 2013-01.
We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS)
facilitate establishment and maintenance of GAD database to have an
effective basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy
formulation and to undertake thorough gender analysis based on
relevant data for proper identification of existing gender issues to
ensure that the approved PPAs to be incorporated in the GPB are
responsive to address the same.
Likewise, we recommended that proper identification and
prioritization of PPAs that would effectively address gender issues and
concerns be conducted and ensure its inclusion in the GAD Plan and
Budget to be implemented during the year.

We recommended that the MPDC and/or Municipal Engineer
properly plan and monitor the implementation of development projects
to maximize the use of 20% development fund for the benefit of the
constituents.
We recommended that the MPDO together with all other officials
and employees concerned to facilitate adequate planning and budgeting
for development projects. Identify the individual PPAs in the AIP and
refrain from inputting lump sum/generic term appropriation. Submit
the required document to prove the validity and legality of the projects
implemented during the year.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and other
concerned municipal officials observe strictly Items 3.0 and 4.0 of Joint
Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 of the DILG and DBM. Avoid
charging expenses which do not constitute capital expenditures under
the 20% Development Fund.
The management committed to comply with the audit
recommendations and the Municipal Mayor also instructed the ICO-
Municipal Accountant to scrutinize every disbursement charged against the
fund prior to processing for payment.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. direct the submission of MSWMP to the Provincial Solid
Waste Management Board for review and for approval by
the National Solid Waste Management Commission;
b. evaluate the current composition of the MSWMB and look
for possible members that could qualify to complete the line
up;
c. stop the operation on open dump site for its garbage disposal,
instead, develop a sanitary landfill as final garbage disposal
site of the LGU; and
d. instruct the municipal officials concerned to review the
budget set for Solid Waste Management for the effective and
efficient performance of its programs and activities.
The solid waste management focal person commented that the
Municipality’s Solid Waste Management Plan was already prepared and
submitted to the DENR on August 11, 2017. Said plan was already forwarded
and received by the National Solid Waste Commission for review. However, he
informed the Audit Team that as per initial evaluation by the ENRO, the plan is

On the municipality’s operation on open dump site, the Municipal Mayor
commented that the Municipality could not afford to develop a sanitary landfill.

convert their open dump site into a categorized land fill until such time that the
municipality has already constructed its own MRF with complete
machineries/equipment for their solid waste management. He further
commented that they will seek financial assistance from National Government
for the realization of this project.

No. 92-389 to prevent the possible re-use of those documents for other claims of

R.A. No. 9184.

Waste Management Board, secure the approval of its 10-year Solid Waste

and make provisions in their plan for the closure or eventual phase out of the
